speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Eli Sommer. Tape number six. What happened to your wife once she was denounced?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was sent to a camp, and she was with Russian girls, she told me. And the Russian girls were very nice to her. And she tried to be with Russian girls. She didn't want to be with the Polish girls because they were the one who denounced her, actually. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow, she-- she-- she-- felt safer with the Russian girls. And that's how she survived, actually, the war with the Russian girls who were in-- in the camp. A working camp, not concentration, but a working camp for the foreigners. But he was very disappointed, this guy, when he found out that after the war, she met him. Because she wanted to-- he always was saying that he wants to adopt her. And she was ready to-- he was ready to adopt her after the war, too. But she would never hear about-- they were very good to her, but they didn't know that she was Jewish. If they would know she was Jewish-- because she was always-- she heard about his outlook about, his opinion about Jews. The worst they could-- they-- they could hear about it. If he knew that she was Jewish, he would of been the first one to denounce her. But they still wanted to-- to-- to adopt her after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you met her, did she-- after you were married-- where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married on Staten Island. We had uncles, which they were not too big help to us. To me financially or whatever to-- in helping. I wanted to learn-- the one of my cousins was a-- had a-- not him, but his father-in-law had a factory. And he was running the factory. And I wanted to learn to [INAUDIBLE]. He gave me a chance to learn the trade, you know how you call it, a cutter. To be a cutter. But they never did. They never-- he said the union wouldn't allow it, the this wouldn't allow it. And so they didn't want to put out themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but they were nice. He invited us for dinner. And they felt that you have a family. I mean, we didn't expect anything, and we didn't receive anything. What are you going to expect? You have [INAUDIBLE] good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so the relationship was good, but they weren't-- they didn't do nothing to really to help me to advance me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they didn't understand what you had gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You know, sometimes words are just words. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Words don't-- you know, when you hear stories about something, you know, it's like history. I mean, it doesn't-- it never affected you, and you have to go through. If you are very sensitive, I imagine, you would do it. You-- there's a lot of people who are really good to family who came in to try to help. And they realized what happened. But they didn't show. You know, its-- it happened. They knew that she had-- they had a family, a sister. She died, and then the whole family vanished. What the-- they-- the brother-- my brother came over here in 1938 after he finished the study in France. And they send him affidavit, and he came in. And he-- they didn't help him that much. You know, they used-- they were pretty well-to-do, and they used influence to give him the-- to get to hospital. At that time, it was very hard to get to the hospital for practice and for the-- to make the justification. But that's about it. Everybody was on his own. And my brother didn't expect. I didn't expect nothing, so I was not disappointed. And that's what. But made-- when they heard that we were intent to get married, and they made sort of a-- my one uncle had a big house. And made-- invited the whole family and the-- for dinner in a restaurant. And then we came to-- to the house. I mean, before it was a wedding in their house. Then they went to a restaurant, then it was went back to the house. It was very nice. It was a nice affair.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married? What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949, that was, I believe. August 5th, 1940-- August 7th, I believe, in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older boy is William, William Henry. He was born 1952. The younger one is Andrew Steven, which was-- he was born in 1956. And they are both are married now. And they both have families, and thanks to God, both doing well. And I'm happy about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have. William has three children. A girl of 19. And a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stacy. And then she has Scott. Was-- he was born two weeks after my late wife died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit later. It was 1981. In February. He was born in March 1981. He's 15 now. 15 and 1/2. And then Jenna, which is the youngest one. She's 11 now. Then the younger one is Andrew, which he has Rachel, who is going to be 12 in December 7. December 7th, which is Pearl Harbor Day. And-- and then she have Hannah, which is eight. Eight, I believe. Oh, I made a mistake. Eight or nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your wife pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last day of February. That was a leap year, 29. February 29, '81.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your current wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her in October. I don't know. Is it 10 or something? At our friends, a mutual friend's wedding-- a daughter's wedding. And we were sitting at the same table. And I guess she was unattached, and I asked her to dance. And from there, we developed. And we got married eventually. We got married a few months-- July 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No-- 19-- oh my God. '82. I am back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] 1982. Jenny. Yeah, Jenny. And we are married already-- what is it? 14 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of your business? You had a business.

speaker: interviewee
text: A business. I had a business later, yes. Sonbell. That was combination of two words. I had a partner, Sonia and-- bell. [LAUGHS] That not too successful. The partner was not right, and I got rid of him. Then I had to struggle with my new business, which I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to my business that I worked before in office supplies, wholesale office supplies. And that's what it is. I was working in this business till my late wife got sick. And that was-- she got-- she was sick for three years. Exactly three years. What message do you have after all that you've gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Message? I guess after-- I'm going to-- to say maybe the same message that we should all get-- get along a little bit better, because we saw what-- what-- what hatred can do to the world. The world was not only to the Jews, but it-- it was terrible war. And Jewish people suffered so much. And-- and I hope that this kind of testimony that we-- I have now here will bode well for the future. That people will see that-- that the only way to get to live in peace and to-- to-- to have a little bit better intention and think a little bit better about human beings as-- as brothers. And-- and another message is for-- for the Holocaust deniers, that they-- they think while we are still alive they can get away to-- to deny-- to deny something. Especially, people usually who didn't know, didn't go through it, that didn't know the history-- but they're just of hatred. They want to deny that-- they just-- what we went through. No, I guess this testimony and-- and-- and the thousands like me would stop these kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that I might have forgotten to ask you that you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess we went through pretty good with everything. I can't think. Maybe I will think about it later, but that will be too late. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Sommer.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. It was a pleasure. This picture was taken in 1928 when my uncle, my mother's brother, came over to Poland for a visit. On the left side is my middle brother, Benneck Baruch in testimony. In the middle is my sister, only sister I had. On the right side, is my other brother, the oldest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the front, I am at age nine. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name was Minka. My brother on the left site is Baruch Benneck. And middle, sister-- my sister, Minka. On the right side is my oldest brother, Monek, Moses, which lives here in the United States. And in the front, I am at age nine. This is a picture of on the left side, that's me, Eli Sommer. On the right side is my sister-- my older sister, Minka. Also taken in 1928 when my uncles from the United States were in Poland. And these are pictures that I received from my uncle after I came over to the United States. They were pictures sent over by my family to United States. This is a picture. On the left side is my grandfather, [NON-ENGLISH]. In the center is my mother, Sedonia. Or you can call it-- the Hebrew name is-- oh, Sedonia. All right. And on the right side is Chaim, Chaim ayosef, my father. Their picture was taken also in 1928 when my uncles from the United States were in Poland visiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: The year and--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in 1931. It was a wedding of my sister to my brother-in-law. Messinger, Minka. My sister, Minka, to my brother-in-law, Motek Messinger. And of course, there were mostly family and friends. And right in front on the-- laying on the-- on the ground, on the floor, that's me, Eli Sommer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 12 years old at that time. This is a picture, which was taken in I believe, 1936 or '37. Standing left to right. Me, Eli Sommer. Then my sister, Minka Sommer. Her husband, Motek Messinger. And my brother which lives now here in the United States, Moses Monek. Sitting from left to right is my mother, Sedonia Sommer. And my niece, younger niece, Zosia Messinger. My father, Chaim ayosef Sommer. And my older niece, Blanka Messinger. It's better here. This is a picture taken probably when I was 16 or 17 in an organization [NON-ENGLISH]. Most of the people are not alive. One friend of mine, next to me. I am right in the center, Eli Sommer. Right in the center. And on the-- on the right of me is Dudeck Schiff, David Schiff, who lives in Israel right now. I don't believe I am-- anybody else. One is [NON-ENGLISH] who lives-- that I heard that lives in Poland, but I really don't know. There I was 16. Around 16, 17 at that time. Here the one-- this picture was taken when I was about 16 or 17. Also on an outing of the Zionist organization, [NON-ENGLISH]. On the left side is here, Eli Sommer. I don't remember where all those people are now. I don't believe there-- any is alive. And that's all I can say. This is a picture when I was 17, I believe. On the right top is Eli Sommer. Right beneath me is David Schiff from Israel. And fourth from the left is still Golda Goldstein-- used to be. I don't remember her now name. They-- Dudeck Schiff and Golda Goldstein are still living in Israel. I don't believe anybody else is alive anymore. And those pictures was taken on the Zionists of [NON-ENGLISH] outing somewhere when I was 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture I got from David Schiff. He was-- during the war, he was in Russia, so he had some pictures with him. And he gave me a copy of it, as the other ones, too. This is a picture identifying me as a representative of the Jewish Information Office in Bayreuth, which was taken in 1945 right after the war in Germany. This is a picture taken in 1969 at the time of my youngest son, Andrew's bar mitzvah. And on the left is Andrew. Next to him is his mother and my wife-- my late wife, Sonia. And I am next to her. And my older son, William, is all the way on the right. This is a picture taken at the time of bar mitzvah-- Scott, a son of William and Helene Sommer-- of my grandson's bar mitzvah. On the left side, starting with the left side is the [BACKGROUND NOISE] wife of my son, Becky Sommer. Next to my son, younger son, Andrew Sommer. In the middle, in the center-- I, Eli Sommer. And bar mitzvah-- next to me is bar mitzvah man, Scott Sommer, son of William and Helene. Now next to him is my son, older son, William. Next to him is older daughter, Stacy Sommer. Coming back, again from the left side, standing is my granddaughter, Rachel. My present wife, Jenny Sommer. Next to her on the right is Hannah Sommer, daughter of Andrew Sommer. And sitting next on the right is Helene Sommer, wife of my-- William Sommer. And next to her on the right, Jenna Sommer, daughter of-- my granddaughter and daughter of William and Helene Sommer.
